A human rights risk management system is a programme to identify, assess, and address human rights risks and impacts within the company’s own operations and supply chain. The risk management system aims at preventing, minimising or ending these risks and impact.

Human rights risks defined by the German Supply Chain Act: • Child Labour • Forced labour, Modern Slavery or Human Trafficking • Occupational Health and Safety, including working hours • Freedom of Association and Collective Bargaining • Equality in employment, including equal wages for equal work • Fair living wages • Pollution of soil / water / air, noise emissions and water consumption • Land rights • Hiring or use of private / public security forces without an adequate command structure or proper oversight to prevent harm • Environmental risks defined by the German Supply Chain Act • The use of mercury and mercury compounds, manufacture of mercury-added products, or the treatment of mercury waste • Production and use of hazardous chemicals • Handling, collection, storage and disposal of waste in a manner that is not aligned with local environmental protection regulation • Export/Import of hazardous waste

Human rights risk analysis in the sense of the German Supply Chain Act is a process in which the company identifies the human rights risks and impacts that may result from its own business operations and within their supply chain. This includes human rights risks arising from environmental risk / impact factors (e.g., pollution of air, water and land, the use of mercury and hazardous chemicals, and the handling of waste, including hazardous waste). The GSCA requires risk analysis to be conducted regularly, namely at least annually.
Does the company regularly undertake risk analysis focused on human rights (including impacts on human rights arising from environmental impacts / risks)?
Yes
No

Direct suppliers are entities with which the company has a contractual agreement for the supply of goods or the provision of services necessary for the productions of your company’s products of provision of services. Where companies attempt to circum-navigate the direct supplier relationship to reduce their obligations, the German Supply Chain Act will deem the relationship as direct, nonetheless.
\nOwn operations encompass subsidiaries or affiliated companies under decisive influence of the parent company. All entities under decisive influence need to be covered by the regular risk analysis.\n

Please click on "more" below for definitions on each human rights element.

**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Child Labour is defined as:** • The employment of a child under the age at which compulsory schooling ends according to the law of the place of employment, provided that the age of employment is not less than 15 years • Any child that at the time of employment is less than 15 years of age, except where the law of the place of employment allows so, provided that it is not a form of slavery, and that it complies with the International Labour Organization’s convention (no.138) concerning the Minimum Age for Admission to Employment
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Forced Labour, Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking are defined as:** • Forced Labour: this includes any work or service that is required of a person under threat of punishment and for which the individual has not made themselves available voluntarily, for examples as a result of debt bondage or trafficking in human beings • Modern Slavery: covers all forms of slavery, practices akin to slavery, or other forms of domination or oppression in the workplace such as extreme economic or sexual exploitation and humiliation
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Occupational Health and Safety is defined as:** • Occupational Health and Safety is ensuring that the place of employment doesn’t have or takes precautions against the risk of accidents at work or work related health hazards. This requires having safety standards in place, the provision of maintenance of the workplace, measures in place to prevent excessive physical and mental fatigue (rest breaks, having decent working hours), and adequate training of employees
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Freedom of Association is defined as:** • Freedom of Association is the right of employees to form or join trade unions which must not be used as a reason for unjust discrimination or retaliation • Trade unions should be free to operate in accordance with applicable law of the place of employment which includes the right to strike and the right to collective bargaining
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, equality in employment, including equal wages for equal work is defined as:** • The unequal treatment in employment on the grounds of national and ethnic origin, social origin, health status, disability, sexual orientation, age, gender, political opinion, religion or belief • An example of unequal treatment is the payment of unequal remuneration for work of equal value
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Adequate Living Wage is defined as:** • Adequate Living Wage amounts to at least the minimum wage as laid down by the applicable law
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Land Rights are defined as:** • The unlawful eviction from and the unlawful taking of land, forests and waters in the acquisition, development or other use of land , forests and waters, the use of which secures the livelihood of a person
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, the Hiring or Use of Security Forces is prohibited if it leads to:** • Torture and cruel, inhumane or degrading treatment • Damages to life or limb • Impairing the right to organise and the freedom of association
**As per the German Supply Chain Act, Pollution of Soil/Water/Air, Noise Emissions and Water Consumption are prohibited if they:** • Significantly impair the natural bases for the preservation and production of food • Denies a person access to safe and clean drinking water • Makes it difficult for a person to access sanitary facilities or destroys them • Harms the health of a person

In addition to a regularly conducted risk analysis, the German Supply Chain Act defines two instances in which companies are required to carry out so called “ad-hoc risk analysis”. The requirement to conduct an “ad-hoc risk analysis” is triggered by (1) a significant change in the risk situation and (2) substantiated knowledge of a possible human rights violation in the indirect supply chain.
Does the company undertake “ad-hoc risk analysis” as defined by the German Supply Chain Act?
Yes
No

Examples of internal business decisions include but are not limited to the introduction of new products, services, projects or business fields; when entering or sourcing from new markets, when establishing new joint ventures. Examples of external events include but are not limited to natural disasters or the outbreak of conflict.
Substantiated knowledge means that the company has indications suggesting that the violation of human rights or of an environmental obligation is possible. A company can gain substantiated knowledge through, for example: complaints raised through its complaints or whistleblowing mechanisms, handouts by the Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control, media reports, reports by NGOs and notifications on the internet if they are common knowledge because they are known industry-wide or are passed on to the enterprise.

An external event or internal business decision that may impact the company’s risk profile in the company’s indirect supply chain triggers an ad-hoc risk analysis

The German Supply Chain Act requires that risks are weighted and prioritised, enabling companies to take a risk-based approach to managing human rights risks. The GSCA also recommends a set of criteria for prioritizing human rights risks.
Can the company demonstrate that risks (identified through the risk analysis) are prioritised?
Yes
No

•	**Severity:** The severity of a risk is based on (a) the scale of the risk (how grave is/would the impact on rightsholders be), (b) the scope of the risk (how many people are/would be affected) and (c) the possibility to remediate the risk (is it possible to return the situation to its original state for the affected rightsholders). 
•	**Likelihood:** The likelihood of a risk refers to how likely it is for a risk to happen. Relevant factors that can reduce or increase likelihood include external factors (e.g., laws and regulations in which a company operates, the socio-economic context, military conflict) and internal factors (e.g., the company’s policies and practices, its control systems and measures to prevent and mitigate risks).
•	**Nature of causal contribution** defines whether the company is directly responsible for (or made a major contribution to) causing a human rights risk / impact, or if it is only partially responsible by contributing to, enabling or incentivising a human rights risk / impact by a third party.

As per the German Supply Chain Act, a policy statement must be a single document fulfilling all criteria required by the law. It can refer to other documents that further spell out individual elements in more detail. \nThe policy statement must contain at least the following elements:

• The description of the due diligence and risk management procedures related to human rights and environment-related risks as listed by the German Supply Chain Act
• To clearly state the priority human rights and environment-related risks identified through the risk analysis 
• To define, based on the risk analysis, the human rights and environment-related expectations placed by the business on its employees and suppliers in the supply chain

This requirement of the German Supply Chain Act aims to ensure that companies act without delay to reduce, prevent and mitigate the identified risks. This applies to risks that companies are directly responsible for (‘cause’) or when they have a major contribution to it (‘contribute’). The higher the risk is prioritized, the more urgent a company is expected to address the risk through adequate measures.  \nWithout undue delay: While a specific time period is not stated in the German Supply Chain Act, businesses are expected to act as early as feasible and within an appropriate amount of time.\n

Remedial actions: when an actual impact or violation is identified, the steps taken to return the situation to its original state to the best of the company's ability are regarded as remedial actions. Remediation goes beyond financial compensation and can include actions like prevention, issuing an apology etc. The remedy should be deemed appropriate by and meet the needs of the people affected. When violations are identified in own operations in Germany, the violation must be ended. In own operations abroad or entities under decisive influence, violations must usually be ended.

The German Supply Chain Act requires that companies continuously document their risk management and due diligence processes, in particular when related to complex considerations and decisions (e.g., how risks are prioritised or about terminating a business relationship due to severe human rights violations). The objective of documenting decisions and processes is to ensure that decisions and their justification can be explained and understood by internal and external stakeholders even in a distant future. The GSCA requires this documentation to be kept available for at least seven years from its creation.
